question: What's the risk on HIGH_142 and what should I do?

agent_responses.response: HIGH_142 hits 118% capacity in 18 minutes. 21 flights against a capacity of 18, three over. 22 flights are converging through the single high-altitude sector.
A convective line is blocking the eastern corridor with echo tops at 44,000 ft and reflectivity up to 52 dBZ. It is forcing eastbound traffic into HIGH_142.
If unmitigated, arrival banks at ORD and EWR inherit roughly 247 minutes of delay, and HIGH_144 pressure rises next.
evidence: ORD and EWR arrival banks exposed

agent_responses.response: 8 similar scenarios on record. A western reroute worked 83% of the time with an average delay reduction of 18%. Closest match is 2025-08-13.
Caution: storm propagation is running 38% faster than the matched precedent, so I have lowered historical confidence from 91% to 87%. The precedent only partially applies.

synthesis.answer: HIGH_142 breaches in 18 minutes, driven by a fast-moving storm pushing traffic east. Recommend metering westbound flow before the peak: it clears the breach to ~96% and cuts delay ~21%, but watch HIGH_144 climbing to ~95%. Confidence 87%, reduced because the storm is outrunning the historical analog.
